graph_model.tree_policy: Trees are extracted views; the underlying model is a directed typed multigraph.

axiom.meaning: Accepted foundational principle used without proof.

assumption.meaning: Hypothesis required for a theorem, proof, construction, or example.

definition.meaning: Exact definition of a mathematical notion.

structure.meaning: Mathematical structure, usually a set with operations, topology, norm, or inner product.

object.meaning: Mathematical item that is neither primarily a theorem nor a definition, such as an operator or functional.

property.meaning: Predicate or qualitative condition carried by structures or objects.

construction.meaning: Procedure or induced object built from other objects.

example.meaning: Item satisfying one or more definitions or illustrating a theorem.

non_example.meaning: Item failing a definition because one or more required conditions fail.

counterexample.meaning: Item showing that a statement or implication fails when assumptions are removed.

corollary.meaning: Result following directly from another result.

proof.meaning: Complete proof object proving a result.

application.meaning: Use of a result in another mathematical or applied context.

definitional_dependency.meaning: Needed to parse, state, or type-check an item.

logical_dependency.meaning: Used as a mathematical result, tool, or proof ingredient.

assumption_dependency.meaning: Hypothesis needed for truth or validity, not merely for notation.

construction_dependency.meaning: Needed to build the object or topology.

pedagogical_dependency.meaning: Useful learning prerequisite, not logically necessary.

defines.meaning: Source supplies the definition of target.

introduces.meaning: Source introduces target as a named item.

assumes.meaning: Source requires target as an explicit hypothesis or axiom.

requires.meaning: Source needs target to be stated, typed, or valid.

uses.meaning: Source uses target as a proof ingredient, tool, method, or standard result.

implies.meaning: Truth of source entails truth of target.

equivalent_to.meaning: Source and target are logically or definitionally equivalent in the stated context.

generalizes.meaning: Source is a broader version of target.

specializes.meaning: Source is a narrower version of target.

subtype_of.meaning: Source is a subtype or richer structure than target.

instance_of.meaning: Source is an instance of target.

inherits_from.meaning: Source inherits operations, topology, or axioms from target.

has_example.meaning: Source is illustrated by example target.

has_counterexample.meaning: Source has target as a counterexample or non-example.

shows_necessity_of.meaning: Source demonstrates why target assumption, property, or theorem hypothesis matters.

motivates.meaning: Source motivates target historically, pedagogically, or conceptually.

prerequisite_for.meaning: Source is a useful learning prerequisite for target.

applied_to.meaning: Source is applied to target context.

part_of.meaning: Source is a part of target.

has_part.meaning: Source has target as a component.

edge_schema.direction_semantics: Each edge is read literally as source --type--> target.

items.label: Functional Analysis
items.definition: Study of infinite-dimensional vector spaces with topology, norm, duality, operators, and limits.
items.intuition: Linear algebra with limiting processes and topology kept visible.

items.label: scalar field
items.definition: The base field, usually real or complex numbers.

items.label: Axiom of Choice
items.statement: Every family of nonempty sets has a choice function.

items.label: Zorn's Lemma
items.statement: Every partially ordered set in which every chain has an upper bound has a maximal element.

items.label: vector space
items.definition: A set with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying the vector space axioms over a scalar field.

items.label: linear subspace
items.definition: A subset of a vector space closed under addition and scalar multiplication.

items.label: metric space
items.definition: A set equipped with a distance function satisfying positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.

items.label: topology
items.definition: A collection of open sets closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.

items.label: topological vector space
items.definition: A vector space with a topology making addition and scalar multiplication continuous.

items.label: normed vector space
items.definition: A vector space equipped with a norm.

items.label: Banach space
items.definition: A normed vector space complete in the metric induced by its norm.

items.label: inner product space
items.definition: A vector space equipped with an inner product inducing a norm.

items.label: Hilbert space
items.definition: An inner product space complete in the norm induced by its inner product.

items.label: locally convex space
items.definition: A topological vector space with a local base at zero consisting of convex sets.

items.label: dual space
items.definition: The space of all continuous linear scalar-valued functionals on a topological vector space; for normed spaces, all bounded linear functionals.

items.label: norm
items.definition: A map ||.||: X -> [0,infty) satisfying positive definiteness, homogeneity, and the triangle inequality.

items.label: metric induced by a norm
items.definition: Given a normed space, define d(x,y)=||x-y||.

items.label: topology induced by a metric
items.definition: The topology generated by open metric balls.

items.label: completeness
items.definition: Every Cauchy sequence converges to a point in the space.

items.label: compactness
items.definition: Every open cover has a finite subcover; in metric spaces, sequential compactness is equivalent.

items.label: closedness
items.definition: A subset contains all its topological limit points, equivalently its complement is open.

items.label: convexity
items.definition: A subset contains every line segment between any two of its points.

items.label: weak topology
items.definition: The weakest topology on X making every element of X* continuous.

items.label: weak-star topology
items.definition: The weakest topology on X* making all evaluation maps x* -> x*(x), x in X, continuous.

items.label: boundedness
items.definition: A set or operator is bounded when its size is controlled by a finite constant in the relevant norm.

items.label: continuity
items.definition: Preimages of open sets are open; for linear maps between normed spaces, equivalent to boundedness.

items.label: surjectivity
items.definition: Every point in the codomain has a preimage.

items.label: open mapping property
items.definition: A map sends open sets to open sets.

items.label: closed graph property
items.definition: The graph {(x,Tx): x in X} is closed in X x Y.

items.label: linear functional
items.definition: A linear map from a vector space to its scalar field.

items.label: linear operator
items.definition: A linear map between vector spaces.

items.label: bounded linear operator
items.definition: A linear operator T between normed spaces with ||Tx|| <= C||x|| for some C and all x.

items.label: continuous linear operator
items.definition: A linear operator continuous in the norm topologies.

items.label: operator norm
items.definition: For a bounded linear operator T, ||T|| = sup{||Tx|| : ||x|| <= 1}.

items.label: compact operator
items.definition: A bounded linear operator sending bounded sets to relatively compact sets.

items.label: adjoint operator
items.definition: For T: X -> Y bounded, T*: Y* -> X* is defined by T*(y*)=y* o T.

items.label: self-adjoint operator
items.definition: An operator T on a Hilbert space satisfying T=T*.

items.label: complete domain assumption
items.statement: The domain space is complete, typically a Banach space.

items.label: complete codomain assumption
items.statement: The codomain space is complete, typically a Banach space.

items.label: surjectivity assumption
items.statement: The operator is onto its codomain.

items.label: closed graph assumption
items.statement: The graph of the linear operator is closed.

items.label: local convexity assumption
items.statement: The topological vector space is locally convex.

items.label: compact self-adjoint assumption
items.statement: The Hilbert-space operator under consideration is compact and self-adjoint.

items.label: dual star notation
items.definition: The star X* denotes the continuous dual space of X.

items.label: operator space notation
items.definition: L(X,Y) denotes bounded linear operators from X to Y.

items.label: weak-star notation
items.definition: w* or sigma(X*,X) denotes the weak-star topology on X*.

items.label: Zorn maximality argument
items.definition: Build a partially ordered family and use Zorn's Lemma to obtain a maximal object.

items.label: Baire category argument
items.definition: Use Baire category to turn a countable covering by closed sets into an interior point or uniform bound.

items.label: diagonal argument
items.definition: Extract or construct sequences by coordinating countably many requirements.

items.label: density argument
items.definition: Prove a statement on a dense subspace and extend by continuity or closure.

items.label: compactness argument
items.definition: Use compactness to pass to convergent subnet, subsequence, or finite subcover.

items.label: duality argument
items.definition: Translate a problem into the dual or bidual where functionals separate points.

items.label: approximation argument
items.definition: Approximate general objects by simpler dense classes or finite-rank objects.

items.label: Baire Category Theorem
items.statement: In a complete metric space, a countable intersection of dense open sets is dense.
items.formal_statement: If (M,d) is complete and U_n are dense open subsets of M, then intersection_n U_n is dense in M.
items.conclusion: Countable intersections of dense open sets remain dense.

items.label: Hahn-Banach Theorem
items.statement: A bounded linear functional on a subspace of a normed space extends to the whole space without increasing its norm.
items.formal_statement: If V <= X and f in V* is bounded, then there exists F in X* with F|_V=f and ||F||=||f||.
items.conclusion: Norm-preserving extension of bounded linear functionals exists.

variants.label: real normed-space form
hypotheses: bounded real linear functional on a subspace
variants.conclusion: There is a norm-preserving real linear extension.

variants.label: complex normed-space form
hypotheses: bounded complex linear functional on a subspace
variants.conclusion: There is a norm-preserving complex linear extension.

items.label: Hahn-Banach Separation Theorem
items.statement: In a locally convex space, a point outside a closed convex set can be separated from it by a continuous linear functional.
items.formal_statement: If E is locally convex, K closed convex, and x0 notin K, then exists f in E* with f(x0)>sup_{y in K} f(y) in the real case.
items.conclusion: Continuous linear functionals separate closed convex sets from exterior points.

items.label: Banach-Steinhaus Theorem / Uniform Boundedness Principle
items.statement: Pointwise bounded families of bounded linear operators on a Banach space are uniformly bounded in operator norm.
items.formal_statement: If X is Banach and {T_i in L(X,Y)} is pointwise bounded, then sup_i ||T_i|| < infinity.
items.conclusion: Pointwise boundedness upgrades to uniform operator-norm boundedness.

items.label: Open Mapping Theorem
items.statement: A bounded surjective linear operator between Banach spaces is open.
items.formal_statement: If X,Y are Banach spaces and T in L(X,Y) is surjective, then T maps open subsets of X to open subsets of Y.
items.conclusion: Surjective bounded linear maps between Banach spaces are open maps.

items.label: Bounded Inverse Theorem
items.statement: A bounded linear bijection between Banach spaces has bounded inverse.
items.formal_statement: If T: X -> Y is a bounded linear bijection and X,Y are Banach, then T^{-1}: Y -> X is bounded.
items.conclusion: Continuous linear bijections between Banach spaces are isomorphisms.

items.label: Closed Graph Theorem
items.statement: A linear operator between Banach spaces with closed graph is bounded.
items.formal_statement: If X,Y are Banach and T: X -> Y is linear with closed graph in X x Y, then T is continuous.
items.conclusion: Closed graph implies continuity for linear maps between Banach spaces.

items.label: Tychonoff Theorem
items.statement: Arbitrary products of compact topological spaces are compact in the product topology.
items.conclusion: Product compactness holds for arbitrary products.

items.label: Banach-Alaoglu Theorem
items.statement: The closed unit ball of the dual space is compact in the weak-star topology.
items.formal_statement: For a normed space X, B_{X*} is compact in sigma(X*,X).
items.conclusion: Dual closed unit balls are weak-star compact.

items.label: Hilbert Projection Theorem
items.statement: Every point in a Hilbert space has a unique nearest point in a nonempty closed convex subset; in particular, closed subspaces have orthogonal projections.

items.label: Riesz Representation Theorem
items.statement: Every bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space is inner product with a unique vector.
items.formal_statement: For Hilbert H and phi in H*, exists unique y in H such that phi(x)=<x,y> for all x in H.
items.conclusion: The Hilbert-space dual is represented by H itself via the inner product.

variants.label: real Hilbert-space variant
variants.conclusion: Every bounded linear functional is x -> <x,y>.

variants.label: complex Hilbert-space variant
variants.conclusion: Every bounded linear functional is represented by a unique vector with the convention-dependent order.

items.label: Spectral Theorem
items.statement: A compact self-adjoint operator on a Hilbert space admits an orthonormal eigenbasis decomposition on the closure of its range, with real spectrum accumulating only at 0.
items.formal_statement: If T is compact self-adjoint on Hilbert H, then nonzero spectrum consists of real eigenvalues of finite multiplicity with 0 as only possible accumulation point, and H decomposes orthogonally into eigenspaces plus ker(T).
items.conclusion: Compact self-adjoint operators behave like diagonalizable symmetric matrices, up to compact-limit effects.

items.label: Proof of Hahn-Banach by Zorn's Lemma
items.statement: Extend a dominated functional maximally and prove the maximal domain is the whole space.
items.strategy: Partially order admissible extensions; use Zorn; rule out proper maximal domain by one-dimensional extension.

items.label: Hahn-Banach proof step: admissible extensions
items.statement: Define the partially ordered set of admissible dominated extensions.
items.claim: Admissible extensions form a partially ordered set under extension.
items.justification: Order by domain inclusion and agreement on the smaller domain.

items.label: Hahn-Banach proof step: chain upper bound
items.statement: The union of a chain of admissible extensions is admissible.
items.claim: Every chain has an upper bound.
items.justification: Linearity and domination are preserved because chain domains are nested.

items.label: Hahn-Banach proof step: maximal extension
items.statement: Apply Zorn's Lemma to obtain a maximal admissible extension.
items.claim: There exists a maximal admissible extension.
items.justification: Zorn's Lemma applies to the ordered family from the previous steps.

items.label: Hahn-Banach proof step: one-dimensional extension
items.statement: If the maximal domain is not all of X, extend by one new direction.
items.claim: A proper maximal domain can be enlarged by choosing one scalar value satisfying the domination inequalities.
items.justification: The Hahn-Banach interval inequality supplies an admissible value on the new vector.

items.label: Hahn-Banach proof step: conclusion
items.statement: The maximal domain must be the whole space.
items.claim: The maximal admissible extension is defined on X and has the same norm.
items.justification: The previous step contradicts maximality unless the maximal domain is all of X.

items.label: Proof of Banach-Steinhaus by Baire category
items.statement: Closed pointwise-bounded level sets cover X; one has interior; linearity gives uniform operator bounds.
items.strategy: Apply Baire category to closed sets A_n={x: sup_i ||T_i x|| <= n}.

items.label: UBP proof step: closed level sets
items.statement: Define closed sets A_n controlling pointwise bounds.
items.claim: The sets A_n are closed and cover X.
items.justification: Continuity of each T_i makes inverse images of closed balls closed; pointwise boundedness gives the cover.

items.label: UBP proof step: Baire interior
items.statement: Use Baire to find A_n with nonempty interior.
items.claim: Some A_n contains a ball after translation.
items.justification: A complete metric space cannot be a countable union of nowhere dense closed sets.

items.label: UBP proof step: uniform operator bound
items.statement: Translate the ball estimate to the unit ball and obtain a uniform norm bound.
items.claim: All operators have norm bounded by the same constant.
items.justification: Linearity converts boundedness on a translated ball into boundedness on the unit ball.

items.label: Proof of Open Mapping Theorem by Baire category
items.statement: Use surjectivity to cover Y by scaled images of the unit ball, then upgrade almost openness to openness.
items.strategy: Baire gives interior to the closure of T(B_X); completeness lets approximate preimages sum to a genuine preimage.

items.label: OMT proof step: Baire on image balls
items.statement: Because T is onto, Y is the union of scaled images of B_X.
items.claim: Some closure of T(B_X) has nonempty interior.
items.justification: Apply Baire to Y = union_n n closure(T(B_X)).

items.label: OMT proof step: almost openness
items.statement: Interior of the image-ball closure gives a ball contained in the closure of T(B_X).
items.claim: There exists delta>0 with delta B_Y subset closure(T(B_X)).
items.justification: Symmetry and convexity of the unit ball move an interior point to zero.

items.label: OMT proof step: lift by convergent series
items.statement: Successive approximations produce a convergent series of preimages.
items.claim: The closed-ball estimate becomes actual openness.
items.justification: Completeness of X lets the approximate preimages converge to an element mapped to the target.

items.label: Proof of Closed Graph Theorem via Bounded Inverse
items.statement: Make the graph a Banach space and apply the bounded inverse theorem to the projection onto X.
items.strategy: The graph is complete; the projection from the graph to X is a bounded bijection; its inverse bounds T.

items.label: CGT proof step: graph is Banach
items.statement: A closed subspace of X x Y is complete.
items.claim: G(T) is a Banach space under the product norm.
items.justification: X x Y is Banach and G(T) is closed by assumption.

items.label: CGT proof step: projection inverse
items.statement: The projection from the graph to X has a bounded inverse.
items.claim: Boundedness of the inverse projection implies boundedness of T.
items.justification: The inverse sends x to (x,Tx), so ||Tx|| is controlled by ||x||.

items.label: Proof of Banach-Alaoglu by Tychonoff
items.statement: Embed the dual unit ball into a product of compact scalar disks and identify it as closed.
items.strategy: Use the product embedding x* -> (x*(x))_{x in X}; Tychonoff gives compactness; linearity and boundedness define a closed subset.

items.label: Alaoglu proof step: product embedding
items.statement: Embed B_{X*} into product_{x in X} ||x|| B_K.
items.justification: Each scalar disk is compact and Tychonoff handles arbitrary products.

items.label: Alaoglu proof step: closed linearity conditions
items.statement: The image consists of functions satisfying linearity and norm bounds, a closed condition in the product topology.
items.claim: The embedded dual ball is closed, hence compact.
items.justification: Linearity equations and norm inequalities are coordinate-closed.

items.label: Proof skeleton of compact self-adjoint spectral theorem
items.statement: Use compactness to obtain extremal spectral data, self-adjointness for orthogonality, and induction/closure for decomposition.
items.strategy: Find eigenvectors by maximizing the quadratic form on compactly controlled sets; decompose orthogonally and iterate.

items.label: Spectral proof step: extremal eigenvalue
items.statement: Compactness produces a nonzero eigenvector associated with an extremal spectral value when T is nonzero.
items.claim: A compact self-adjoint operator has a nonzero eigenvalue when appropriate.
items.justification: A maximizing sequence has a compactly convergent image; self-adjointness turns extremality into an eigenvector equation.

items.label: Spectral proof step: orthogonal decomposition
items.statement: Eigenspaces for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal and invariant complements reduce the problem.
items.claim: The Hilbert space decomposes into orthogonal invariant pieces.
items.justification: Self-adjointness gives orthogonality; completeness preserves closed orthogonal complements.

items.label: Spectral proof step: compact accumulation
items.statement: Compactness prevents nonzero eigenvalues from accumulating away from zero.
items.claim: Only 0 can be an accumulation point of the nonzero spectrum.
items.justification: An infinite separated sequence of normalized eigenvectors would have images with no convergent subsequence, contradicting compactness.

items.label: ell_p spaces
items.definition: Sequence spaces of p-summable scalar sequences, with p-norm for 1 <= p < infinity; ell_infty is bounded sequences.

items.label: L_p spaces
items.definition: Equivalence classes of p-integrable measurable functions with the Lp norm for 1 <= p <= infinity.

items.label: C(K)
items.definition: Continuous scalar-valued functions on compact K with the supremum norm.

items.label: c_0
items.definition: The Banach space of scalar sequences converging to zero with the supremum norm.

items.label: ell_2 as Hilbert space
items.definition: The square-summable sequence space ell^2 with inner product <x,y>=sum_n x_n conjugate(y_n).

items.label: incomplete normed spaces as non-examples of Banach spaces
items.definition: Normed vector spaces containing Cauchy sequences with no limit in the space.

items.label: unbounded closed-graph operator on incomplete domain
items.statement: The derivative D:C^1[0,1] -> C[0,1] with the sup norm on C^1 has closed graph but is not bounded because the domain is not Banach.

items.label: application to differential equations
items.statement: Functional analytic operator theory turns differential and integral equations into questions about operators on Banach or Hilbert spaces.

edges.label: vector spaces are over scalar fields
dual of normed space is Banach
for linear operators between normed spaces
on a dual space relative to a predual

concept_map.description: Broad conceptual view connecting structures, operators, examples, and major theorems.
concept_map.example_traversal: functional_analysis -> introduces/requires/motivates edges; expand one hop through structures and theorems.

definition_dependency_graph.description: Definitions needed to state other definitions, structures, operators, and theorem statements.
definition_dependency_graph.example_traversal: From spectral_theorem follow outgoing requires edges with dependency_class=definitional_dependency.

theorem_dependency_graph.description: Theorems and logical tools used by other theorems.
theorem_dependency_graph.example_traversal: From open_mapping_theorem follow uses to baire_category_theorem, then assumptions to completeness.

proof_graph.description: Proof objects, their steps, proof methods, and the theorem proved.
proof_graph.example_traversal: From a proof follow has_part to proof steps and uses to methods/results; follow proves to the theorem.

inheritance_tree.description: Tree-like view extracted from subtype and inheritance edges among mathematical structures.
inheritance_tree.example_traversal: Reverse subtype_of from vector_space to topological_vector_space, normed_vector_space, Banach space, inner product space, Hilbert space.

construction_graph.description: How topologies, metrics, duals, and adjoints are constructed from earlier objects.
construction_graph.example_traversal: norm -> metric_induced_by_norm -> topology_induced_by_metric, and bounded_linear_operator -> adjoint_operator.

prerequisite_map.description: Pedagogical learning dependencies, not necessarily logical dependencies.

application_map.description: Theorems and operators connected to applied contexts.
application_map.example_traversal: application_to_differential_equations -> uses spectral_theorem and compact_operator; reverse applied_to finds relevant results.

query_model.description: Queries are graph traversals over item IDs with filters on edge type and dependency class. Definitional, logical, assumption, proof, and pedagogical dependencies should be queried separately unless a combined view is desired.

traversal_conventions.outgoing: Follow edges literally from source to target.
traversal_conventions.incoming: Follow edges in reverse, useful for examples, prerequisites, and inheritance children.
traversal_conventions.both: Allow both directions, useful for path search through mixed views.
traversal_conventions.deduplication: Return unique item IDs unless the user asks for all parallel edges.
traversal_conventions.transitive_closure: Use edge_types marked transitive, such as subtype_of, requires, prerequisite_for, and implies, when the query asks for full closure.

dependency_distinctions.definitions_required_to_state: outgoing requires/constructed_from/defines with dependency_class=definitional_dependency or notation_dependency
dependency_distinctions.assumptions_needed_for_truth: outgoing assumes or requires with dependency_class=assumption_dependency
dependency_distinctions.pedagogical_prerequisites: incoming or outgoing prerequisite_for with dependency_class=pedagogical_dependency

example_queries.example: Start at spectral_theorem. Traverse outgoing requires for statement dependencies, outgoing assumes for hypotheses, and outgoing uses for logical tools.
Start at banach_steinhaus_theorem and follow outgoing requires with dependency_class=definitional_dependency.
From spectral_theorem traverse incoming proves to proof_spectral_compact_self_adjoint, then outgoing uses to proof_method nodes.
Start at open_mapping_theorem and follow outgoing assumes edges.
Start at banach_space. Follow outgoing has_example or incoming instance_of from examples.
Start at complete_domain_assumption. Follow incoming violates_assumption to counterexamples; then follow outgoing shows_necessity_of to affected theorem.
Start at vector_space and traverse incoming subtype_of transitively to recover the extracted inheritance tree.

One path: vector_space -> prerequisite_for normed_vector_space -> prerequisite_for banach_space -> prerequisite_for hilbert_space -> prerequisite_for adjoint_operator -> prerequisite_for self_adjoint_operator -> prerequisite_for spectral_theorem.
